mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno (Policy Engine)
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows users to manage policies as code for security, compliance, and governance. It is particularly useful for teams looking to enforce policies without needing to learn a new language. Key trade-offs include the need for familiarity with Kubernetes resources and potential performance impacts depending on policy complexity.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Kyverno Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno

```

> This is the official repository for Kyverno.

steps.title: Install Kyverno
steps.description:
This command installs Kyverno in the 'kyverno' namespace using the specified version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kyverno

helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

```

> Create a namespace for Kyverno.
> Install Kyverno with the specified version.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Kyverno pods are running correctly in the 'kyverno' namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Ensure all Kyverno pods are in the 'Running' state.

steps.title: Create an example policy
steps.description:
This step creates a sample policy to demonstrate Kyverno's functionality.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kyverno/kyverno/main/docs/examples/policies/policy.yaml

```

> Apply an example policy to test Kyverno.

steps.title: Verify the policy is applied
steps.description:
Check the status of the applied policy to ensure it is working as expected.

```bash

kubectl get cpol -n kyverno

```

> List the cluster policies to verify the example policy is present.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Kyverno
uninstall.description:
This step will remove Kyverno and all associated resources. Be aware that any policies created will also be deleted.

```bash

helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno

kubectl delete namespace kyverno

```

> Uninstall the Kyverno release.
> Delete the Kyverno namespace and all its resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your policies. This step will upgrade Kyverno to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get cpol -n kyverno -o yaml > backup-policies.yaml

helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --namespace kyverno --version 1.17.1

kubectl get pods -n kyverno

```

> Backup existing policies.
> Upgrade to the specified version.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get cpol -n kyverno -o yaml > backup-policies.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration in policies or insufficient resources.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Kyverno pod using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno' for details.

troubleshooting.title: Policies are not being enforced
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The policies may not be applied correctly or the Kyverno controller may not be running.

**Fix:**
Ensure the policies are applied with 'kubectl get cpol -n kyverno' and check the pod status.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to recognize' when applying a policy
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error indicates that the policy is using an unsupported API version.

**Fix:**
Check the API version in the policy YAML and ensure it matches the supported versions.

**Affected versions:** <= 1.12

troubleshooting.title: Kyverno CLI commands fail with 'not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the Kyverno CLI is not installed or not in the PATH.

**Fix:**
Install the Kyverno CLI or ensure it is correctly added to your PATH.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new policy validation features and improved error messages.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.16.x, review the new policy format requirements.

resolution.summary: You should see the Kyverno pods running with 'kubectl get pods -n kyverno' showing all pods in 'Running' state.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
